text.title: Contract-First Development Workflow
text.summary: The six-step contract-first lifecycle: design, review, generate, implement, validate, deploy — with spec as the single source of truth throughout.

rule.statement: Follow the contract-first order: design spec → review spec (treat as a code PR) → generate stubs + clients → implement business logic → validate implementation against spec → deploy. Never write implementation code before the spec is approved.
rule.rationale: Writing code before the contract is approved locks the API shape prematurely. Reviewing spec-first exposes interface problems (missing fields, wrong HTTP verbs, unclear error shapes) before they are encoded in implementation that is expensive to change.

list.item: 1. Design: write OpenAPI spec for the new feature
2. Review: spec PR reviewed by frontend, backend, and API consumers
3. Generate: server stubs, typed client SDKs, test scaffolding from spec
4. Implement: fill in business logic in generated stubs only
5. Validate: run openapi-core contract tests against running server
6. Deploy: spec and implementation are in sync

section.title: Design Phase

rule.statement: OpenAPI spec must define operationId for every endpoint, use $ref for all reusable schemas, and include all response codes including 400 and 500 via shared response references.
rule.rationale: operationId is required for meaningful code generation (method names in clients). $ref prevents schema duplication and keeps the spec maintainable. Documenting error codes in the spec generates typed error handling in SDK clients.

section.reference: OpenAPI 3.1 base spec with Payment API example

rule.statement: Treat OpenAPI spec changes as code changes: require at least one reviewer from each side of the contract (frontend/client and backend/server). Breaking changes must be flagged explicitly in the PR description.
rule.rationale: Silent breaking changes (renaming fields, changing types, removing endpoints) break deployed clients. Review gates make breaking changes deliberate and documentable.

antipattern.description: Updating the spec after the implementation is already deployed.
antipattern.reason: Spec becomes documentation-after-the-fact; it will drift from reality whenever implementation is changed without updating the spec.
